name: Fillmore
short_description: Public BLM rockhounding near Fillmore in central Utah's volcanic plateau. Find agate, jasper, and chalcedony specimens in washes and hillsides. No permit required, personal use only.
description:
Fillmore sits in the heart of Utah's Basin and Range province, where extensive Tertiary volcanic activity created ideal conditions for silica-rich mineral deposits. The surrounding BLM land offers excellent opportunities to collect agate, jasper, chalcedony, and quartz specimens in a high desert setting with open access and minimal restrictions.

This area is characterized by volcanic terrain with exposed rhyolitic and basaltic formations that weather to reveal colorful agates and jasper in nearby washes and along hillsides. Rockhounders commonly find nodules and rounded specimens in sandy washes after seasonal runoff, as well as quality material exposed in weathered volcanic outcrops on adjacent slopes.

The landscape around Fillmore is relatively open and accessible, with good visibility for scanning hillsides and wash gravels. The elevation keeps temperatures moderate year-round during optimal collecting seasons, though summer heat requires careful water planning. The terrain is forgiving enough for beginners but offers enough geological complexity to keep experienced collectors engaged.

Because this is BLM land, access is straightforward: no permits required, no fees, and personal use collection is encouraged within the 25-pound daily limit. The area sees moderate rockhounding pressure, so finding quality material requires systematic searching of washes and using geology knowledge to target promising terrain.

Best results come from focusing on the wash bottoms after rain events when fresh material washes down, and from examining exposed volcanic hillsides where weathering naturally exposes agate and jasper nodules. Bring plenty of water, sun protection, and tools for breaking field specimens.
history: The Fillmore area lies within the Sevier Plateau and Basin and Range transition zone, where Tertiary volcanic activity (35-45 million years ago) deposited thick sequences of rhyolite, andesite, and basalt. Subsequent weathering and erosion over millions of years created the agate and jasper deposits found in modern washes and hillsides, as silica-rich groundwater precipitated minerals into cavities and nodules.
tips: Search the sandy and gravelly bottoms of seasonal washes running south and east from the volcanic hills, where heavy rains naturally concentrate lighter specimens. Use a 10x loupe to identify chalcedony banding and color patterns before collecting, as many promising-looking pieces contain only common quartz. Target weathered volcanic outcrops on south-facing slopes in the morning when backlighting reveals translucent agate specimens. Bring a geological hammer or chisel to extract specimens from harder volcanic matrix, and always break material along natural planes to avoid wasting specimens.
terrain_notes: Rolling high desert terrain at approximately 5,200-5,500 feet elevation. Volcanic hillsides with exposed rhyolite and basalt formations. Washes and bajadas with sandy to rocky footing. Vegetation sparse: low sagebrush, scattered pinyon-juniper. Generally 0.5 to 3 miles from parking areas on foot. Footing is variable: loose gravel in washes, firmer ground on hillsides. No shade available; full sun exposure.
best_season: October - April. The mild fall and spring temperatures make for comfortable rockhounding and easier water management. Winter is possible but occasional snow can block roads. Summer heat (May-September) is intense at this elevation and makes sustained field work difficult; early morning only collecting is recommended if visiting during hot months.
written_directions: From Fillmore, take Main Street (US 89) south toward Meadow for approximately 3-4 miles. Look for BLM land access points and informal pullouts on both sides of the highway near volcanic outcrops and wash crossings. The most accessible sites are within 2 miles south of Fillmore's main commercial area. For more direct access to wash systems and hillside exposures, take local roads or county spurs east toward the volcanic highlands; consult a BLM map or county road atlas for specific unnamed road access points.
road_conditions: Mostly paved highway (US 89) with well-maintained access. Secondary access roads may be unpaved but generally passable in dry conditions with standard vehicles. Some wash crossings may require high-clearance vehicles after rain.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and wide shoulders along US 89. BLM-designated areas may have simple parking areas marked by cairns or signs. Capacity varies by location; expect to find parking within 0.5 miles of productive collecting areas.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Rockhounding is permitted on BLM land without advance notice or permits. Respect any posted closure areas and private property boundaries.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No advanced permits required
Respect posted closures and private land
nearest_services: Fillmore, approximately 3-4 miles north. Full services including gas, food, water, and lodging. Delta, approximately 25 miles southeast, offers additional services.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to rolling terrain accessible to most hikers wearing sturdy footwear. Informal parking areas near US 89 provide close access to productive washes. Hillside collecting requires climbing 200-500 feet of variable slope. Rocky wash bottoms have uneven footing; normal hiking boots recommended. No wheelchair access to collecting areas. Well-hydrated individuals of moderate fitness can access prime sites.

faq.question: What types of agates and jasper can I find rockhounding near Fillmore, Utah?
faq.answer: The Fillmore BLM area yields colorful agate and jasper specimens, including banded chalcedony agate with white, brown, and red striping. Jasper here tends toward red and brown varieties. Specimens range from small pea-sized nodules to hand-sized pieces. Quartz and chalcedony are also common. Concentrating your search in washes and on weathered volcanic hillsides increases your chances of finding quality material.

faq.question: Is rockhounding free at Fillmore, Utah, and do I need a permit?
faq.answer: Yes, rockhounding at Fillmore is completely free on public BLM land. No permit is required for recreational collecting. You simply follow the Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA) rules: personal use only, maximum 25 lbs per day, no commercial collection, and no motorized excavation. Just show up and start collecting.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding near Fillmore?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal for Fillmore rockhounding. Fall and spring offer comfortable temperatures and easier water management. Winter is possible but snow occasionally blocks access. Summer (May-September) brings intense heat at 5,200+ feet elevation, making extended collecting uncomfortable. If visiting summer, plan for early morning collection only and bring plenty of water.

faq.question: How far is Fillmore rockhounding from town, and what tools do I need?
faq.answer: The best BLM collecting areas are within 2-4 miles south of Fillmore proper, accessed from US 89. Most productive sites are within walking distance (0.5 to 3 miles) of pullouts. Bring a geological hammer or chisel for breaking specimens from volcanic matrix, a 10x hand loupe for identifying quality material, a backpack, and plenty of water. Sturdy hiking boots are essential for rocky washes and hillside terrain.

faq.question: Is Fillmore rockhounding good for beginners and families?
faq.answer: Yes, Fillmore is excellent for beginners and families. The terrain is relatively gentle, access is straightforward (no permits), and finding specimens does not require advanced skills. Children as young as 6 can enjoy searching washes and hillsides with adult supervision. The open desert landscape is safe, and the moderate distances from parking keep families from overcommitting. Start in washes where specimens are easier to spot.

faq.question: Where exactly should I search for agate at Fillmore, and how do I identify quality material?
faq.answer: Focus your search in sandy and gravelly seasonal washes where rain events concentrate lighter specimens downslope. Also target south-facing volcanic hillsides where weathering naturally exposes agate and jasper nodules. Use a 10x loupe to look for translucent chalcedony banding, color variation, and smooth interior surfaces that indicate quality agate. Avoid gray-white quartz chunks without banding. Break suspected specimens along natural planes to reveal the interior color and pattern before committing to filling your 25-lb daily limit.
